message.content: Adversaries may exploit browser extensions to gain persistent access to victim systems. These small programs, which enhance browser functionality, can be installed directly or via app stores, often with extensive permissions. Malicious extensions can be introduced through deceptive downloads, social engineering, or pre-existing system compromises. Security measures on browser app stores may be insufficient, allowing malicious extensions to bypass automated scans. Adversaries can manipulate extension update URLs or mobile configuration files to install additional extensions. On macOS 11+, user interaction is required to install .mobileconfig files, reducing silent installation risks. Once installed, malicious extensions can steal user-entered information, including credentials, and act as installers for remote access tools (RATs). They can also facilitate command and control operations, as seen with botnets and malware like Bundlore, Grandoreiro, Kimsuky, and OSX/Shlayer. Mitigation strategies include restricting extension installations, employing browser security settings, monitoring for unusual browser activity, and educating users on the risks of installing unverified extensions.
